sources: Alpine guiding services and climbing blogs

routes.name: Northeast Buttress

watch_out.description: Continuous rockfall hazard on descent, especially late season when steep rock is exposed. Multiple documented incidents of rockfall debris.
Colchuck Glacier becomes icy by mid-season; requires crampons and ice axe. Approach crosses pocket glacier. Late season descent is extremely icy (bare ice in August).
watch_out.seasonal_note: Best in July when glacier has snow cover; dangerous in August when fully iced

watch_out.description: 8,705 ft altitude, exposed ridge. Rapid alpine weather changes common in afternoon. Thunderstorms build quickly, especially mid-summer.

Route not always obvious, especially on descent. Requires route-finding experience. Multiple variations (Beckey route vs. Kearney route). Numerous rappel anchors require coordination with parties and location finding.
watch_out.error_exposure: High on descent; wrong route leads into dangerous terrain

watch_out.description: 4th class scrambling sections mixed with 5.8 climbing. Sustained vertical with continuous significant exposure.
watch_out.terrain: Solid rock in most areas but some loose sections reported on descent

descent.difficulty: Icy and dangerous late season (August+); requires excellent crampon technique
descent.hazards: Bare ice runout, avalanche terrain on approach, rockfall
descent.notes: Descent is most dangerous section; ice conditions critical

routes.turnaround_strategy: Aim to be off Colchuck Glacier by early afternoon to avoid afternoon rockfall and weather. Consider turnaround time 2pm latest.

routes.name: Northeast Couloir

watch_out.description: Narrow couloir (10-50 ft width) with slopes 35-45 degrees, prime avalanche terrain. 1200 ft vertical drop with terrain traps and cliffs. February 2023 avalanche killed 3 climbers when small wet slide (1.5 D-scale) created long traumatic fall through rocks and narrow walled chokes.
watch_out.frequency: Human-triggered possible in moderate stability (Feb 2023 incident)
watch_out.severity: EXTREME - potential fatal fall due to terrain features
watch_out.specific_terrain: Narrow gully with frequent cliffs (10-50 ft bands), rocks, and tight chokes create terrain traps

watch_out.description: Entry and exit can be difficult with snow or ice to 60 degrees. Icy crux in first 1000 ft. Late season ice is dangerous and requires 2+ tools, ice screws, rope.
watch_out.conditions_dependent: Highly variable - can be moderate snow climb or thin ice/mixed
watch_out.timing: Usually retains continuous snow until late May/early June

watch_out.description: Rime formations on exit. Ice can break off couloir walls. Wet avalanche debris (boulders and car-sized ice blocks) documented.
watch_out.frequency: Spring/early summer when wet conditions occur

watch_out.description: Navigating narrow gully, especially in whiteout. No obvious exit route.
South-facing couloir, afternoon sun increases wet-slide risk in spring/early summer.
watch_out.timing: Critical to climb early in day and in cool conditions

descent.difficulty: Very dangerous descent; high avalanche risk with new sloughs
descent.hazards: Avalanche, steep icy slopes, terrain traps
descent.notes: Expert-only descent; many parties rappel or traverse out

seasonal_recommendations.best_window: Late May-early June (before continuous snow melts)
seasonal_recommendations.note: Winter-only technical route per Mountain Project; must have stable freezing
seasonal_recommendations.avoid: Spring when wet avalanche risk elevated; late season when exposed ice

routes.turnaround_strategy: Climb early morning in cold, stable conditions. Turnaround hard stop 10am at latest due to afternoon warming and avalanche risk. If avalanche conditions rated moderate or higher, avoid entirely.

routes.name: North Buttress Couloir

watch_out.description: Wet avalanche hazard documented. May 2018: massive wet snow avalanche consumed descent route for 10+ minutes, carrying boulders and human-sized ice blocks. Route requires cool freeze conditions.
watch_out.conditions: Wet snow avalanches likely in warm conditions; most dangerous spring/early summer

watch_out.description: Fall on steepest sections leads to long slide back toward lake with potential collision with rocks.
First 1000 ft has icy crux requiring 2+ tools, rope, and ice screws.
Route transitions from snow/ice lower to rock/snow higher. Unconsolidated snow covering moderate rock presents mixed climbing hazards.
3500 ft of elevation gain to notch exposes climbers to weather. Rapid alpine afternoon buildups.

descent.difficulty: Serious ski descent; couloir remains steep throughout

seasonal_recommendations.note: Winter route; requires excellent freeze conditions
seasonal_recommendations.avoid: Warm spring/early summer (wet avalanche risk), mid-summer (exposed ice)

routes.turnaround_strategy: Only climb in cool conditions with good freeze forecast. Climb early; turnaround by 10am. Abandon if conditions show warming.

routes.name: Holsten-Hilden

watch_out.description: M6 chimney pitches with unconsolidated snow covering moderate rock. Requires advanced ice/mixed climbing skills. Tricky wide crack (M6) near summit adds technical difficulty.
watch_out.pitches: 2 pitches M6 chimney, 1 pitch M6 wide crack

watch_out.description: Two pitches of AI3+ ice on northeast face. Conditions-dependent for quality and protection. Established in January favorable conditions.
Snow sections covering moderate rock present unstable climbing and poor protection. Several hundred feet of 50-60 degree snow/snow-covered terrain.
Multiple snow fields and ice sections. Right-side approach to Northeast Couloir zone carries avalanche exposure.
First ascent team encountered darkness requiring headlamp navigation for final pitches. Wind exposure significant on summit. Rime-covered conditions documented.
New route with limited ascent history. Navigation between ice, snow, and rock sections not obvious. Traverse left required in middle section.

descent.notes: Requires reversing mixed sections or finding alternate descent

seasonal_recommendations.note: Winter/early spring only; requires uncommon stable/dry conditions
seasonal_recommendations.conditions: Established in 'uncommonly long period of high pressure'

routes.turnaround_strategy: Expert climbers only. Climb early morning to maximize daylight. Have headlamp ready for descent. Monitor weather strictly - first sign of deterioration requires immediate descent.

routes.name: Backbone Ridge (with Fin Direct)
routes.route_type: Alpine rock with mixed snow/ice passages

watch_out.description: Notorious 70-foot, 6-inch-wide offwidth crack. First business pitch at 5.9. Technical challenge requiring offwidth technique.
Loose rock on many sections reported by multiple sources. Vague route and inconsistent steepness. Loose material on descent rappels. Rock quality varies significantly.
watch_out.impact: Delicate footwork required; rockfall hazard above climbers

watch_out.description: Upper section features exposed pitches on The Fin with fearsome exposure - views straight down thousands of feet to Colchuck Lake. Rotten rock adjacent to 45-degree snow gully. Lowered off positions from poorly directed slings on tiny flakes.
watch_out.exposure: EXTREME - vertical exposure thousands of feet
watch_out.rock_quality: Most rotten rock possible, short of total scree

watch_out.description: Vague route prominence. Not well-defined line. Multiple variations exist. Finding rappel anchors requires coordination and location finding.
watch_out.error_exposure: High - wrong route leads into exposed terrain

watch_out.description: Gully behind fin features soft 45-degree snow pasted over rotten rock. Late-season ice conditions.
9,010 ft altitude with sustained exposure. Afternoon thunderstorms common in summer.
Multiple documented climber injuries on this route 'under various circumstances' per experienced guides.

descent.route: Rappel descent requiring finding and coordinating anchors
descent.hazards: Loose rock on rappels, poor anchor placement, exposure
descent.notes: Most dangerous section; requires excellent coordination

seasonal_recommendations.avoid: Early season (snow/ice on Fin), late season (loose rock, poor conditions)

routes.turnaround_strategy: Break route into 2-3 parts mentally. Aim to be off Fin by 2pm. Late-season ice on descent snow field requires specialized gear - consider turnaround if conditions not known.

routes.name: Triple Couloirs

watch_out.description: Three separate couloirs with rock bands between. Deep couloirs contain exposure but avalanche risk still present. Second couloir described as 'straight up middle of peak with exposure that makes anyone question sanity of ski descent.' Spring climbing season brings wet slide potential.
watch_out.terrain: 3 narrow gullies separated by short steep rock bands
watch_out.avalanche_risk: Moderate to high depending on conditions
watch_out.seasonal: Highest spring when wet slides possible

watch_out.description: 70-80 degree water ice on all three couloirs. WI3 rating. Sustained ice climbing over 2500 vertical feet. Conditions-dependent; ice quality varies.
watch_out.ice_quality: Water ice - good protection but exposed

watch_out.description: Short steep rock bands between couloirs. M3+ mixed ground requires rock protection and mixed climbing skills.
20-pitch route on north face of 9010 ft peak. Afternoon weather buildup common. Long exposure window increases weather risk.
watch_out.duration: Full climbing day on exposed face

watch_out.description: Return via same couloirs (descent avalanche risk) or traverse out. Most parties descend route. Some have skied descent (noted as questionable judgment).
Route length may push into evening hours. Darkness in couloir, limited light for finding rappel anchors.

descent.difficulty: Avalanche risk on descent. Second couloir particularly exposed.
descent.notes: Ski descent noted as questionable despite documented attempts

seasonal_recommendations.avoid: Mid-summer (afternoon thunderstorms, wet ice), winter (extreme cold)

routes.turnaround_strategy: Climb early morning. Aim to be off second couloir by 1pm to avoid afternoon weather. If above-timberline weather building, descend immediately. Route length precludes fast retreat.

routes.name: East Ridge via Aasgard Pass Route

watch_out.hazard: Steep snow slope to Aasgard Pass - avalanche potential
watch_out.description: 35-degree snow slope, 700 ft climb to 8500 ft saddle. Beware of avalanche danger if snow covered. Choice of route up slope affects safety.
Snow slope turns to ice in late season, significantly increasing difficulty. Crampons, ice axe required. Slipping hazard high.
Documented serious accidents from glissading descent from Aasgard Pass. High-speed fall potential on steep icy slope with crevasse/waterfall hazards at base.
watch_out.season: Late season (ice) and early season (moderate angle snow)

watch_out.description: Steep snow, firm runouts below Aasgard Pass. Moats (gaps between snow and rocks) create fall hazards and route-finding confusion.
watch_out.season: All season but worst late summer

watch_out.description: Above Aasgard Pass, late-season rockfall increases as snow recedes. Documented incidents of climbers hit by rockfall on Colchuck Glacier.

Route-finding from pass up to summit via loose talus and boulder fields. No obvious line. Cairns primary guide but shift year-to-year with snowfield recession. 'Distinctive boulders' and landmarks key to return route.
watch_out.visibility_impact: Dangerous in whiteout; loose talus cascades underfoot

watch_out.description: Upper descent must not drift 'too far climber's right (West) into steep cliffed-out terrain near waterfall complex.' Significant drop-off exposure with fatal consequences.
watch_out.severity: HIGH - wrong direction leads to cliffs
watch_out.landmarks: Must identify waterfall complex and avoid western drifting

watch_out.description: Fast-building thunderstorms common. Whiteout navigation in Core Enchantments zone. 9010 ft altitude, sustained exposure.
watch_out.visibility_impact: Whiteout disorientation in upper basin common

watch_out.description: Final descent via loose talus/scree that 'can cascade beneath your feet.' Low angle but unstable.

descent.route: Reverse route - retrace to Aasgard Pass, down snow slope
descent.difficulty: Glissading descent is dangerous; many control descent
descent.hazards: Avalanche, ice, glissading accidents, crevasse fall, waterfall holes
descent.notes: Descent most dangerous section - avoid glissading if any doubt about control

seasonal_recommendations.early_season: April-June - consequential snow/ice, moats, glissading danger
seasonal_recommendations.late_season: October+ - loose scree, icy passages, shorter daylight

routes.turnaround_strategy: Non-negotiable turnaround: If Aasgard Pass not reached by 10am, descend immediately. Afternoon thunderstorms mandatory turn-around signal. If snow icy or glissading control doubtful, take time to control descent regardless of darkness.

routes.name: Serpentine Arete

watch_out.description: Loose rock through much of low 5th class climbing. Approximately 3 pitches of 'terribly loose scrambling.' Delicate movements essential. Rockfalls can occur above climbers.
watch_out.impact: Slows climbing, increases exposure time; requires continuous vigilance

watch_out.description: Route-finding on upper section 'difficult.' Summit direction not obvious. Multiple 4th to mid-5th class options create navigation confusion.
watch_out.error_exposure: High - wrong route leads into more technical terrain

watch_out.description: Right-trending crack traverse at mid-5th class with 'good exposure.'
9010 ft altitude, sustained exposure through multiple pitches. Afternoon thunderstorm risk mid-summer.

watch_out.hazard: Transition from snow to rock time-sink
watch_out.description: Initial transition from snow onto base of route can be major time killer, extending overall day length.
Large objective with significant getting-benighted potential noted in climbing community.

descent.difficulty: Complex due to loose rock and exposure
descent.equipment: Crampons and ice axe essential for descent

seasonal_recommendations.avoid: Early season (snow cover, mixed terrain), late season (loose rock, icy passages)

routes.turnaround_strategy: Hard turnaround: 2pm at latest. If not making good progress by pitch 8-9, consider descent. Route length and loose rock make fast retreat difficult. Mandatory helmet. Have headlamp accessible in case of benightment.
